coredata.dc:title: A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ying
Qu, Zhi-bei
Zeng, Yanbo
Zhou, Tianshu
Shi, Guoyue
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized for determination of paranitrophenol (4-NP) in water sample, where molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was incorporated in GQDs-based sensing system for the first time. A simple hydrothermal method was used to fabricate silica-coated GQDs. The final composite was developed by anchoring the MIP layer on the silica-coated GQDs using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as functional monomer and tetraethoxysilane as crosslinker. The combination of GQDs and MIP endows the composite with stable fluorescent property and template selectivity. Due to resonance energy transfer from GQDs (donor) to 4-NP (acceptor), the fluorescence of the MIP-coated GQDs composite can be efficiently quenched when 4-NP molecules rebound to the binding sites. The composite was applied to the detection of the non-emissive 4-NP and exhibited a good linearity in range of 0.02–3.00µgmL−1 with the detection limit of 9.00ngmL−1 (S/N=3). This work may open a new possibility for developing GQDs-based composite with selective recognition, and it is desirable for chemical sensing application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Fluorescent sensor
4-NP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIP- and NIP-coated GQDs
TEM characterization of silica-coated GQD, MIP-coated GQDs
Absorption and photoluminescence properties of silica- and MIP-coated GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of GO (a), APTS-GO (b), silica-coated GQDs (c), MIP-coated GQDs (d); TEM images of silica-coated GQDs (B) and MIP-coated GQDs (C).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NIP-coated GQDs (a), MIP-coated GQDs before (d) and after (b) template molecules extraction, MIP-coated GQDs (16mgmL−1) with addition of 2.00μgmL−1 4-NP (c); (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP (curve a) and emission spectrum of MIP-coated GQDs (curve b) at pH 9.0 (condition: 0.1molL−1 Na2CO3–NaHCO3); and (C) schematic illustration of the MIP-coated GQDs sensor for 4-NP detection.

Fluorescence emission spectra MIP-coated GQDs (A) and NIP-coated GQDs (B) with addition of indicated concentrations of 4-NP in Na2CO3–NaHCO3 (0.1molL−1, pH 9.0); Stern–Volmer plots (C) from MIP- and NIP-coated GQDs with 4-NP; concentration of MIP- and NIP-coated GQDs is 16mgmL−1.
Illustration of the preparation of MIP-coated GQDs.
The comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods of 4-NP determination.

p-NiTSPc a coated carbon fiber microelectrode

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 4-NP is cited in the list of pollutants of the U.S. EPA which has set the allowed limit of 4-NP in drinking water at 60ngmL−1, LOD: Limit of detection.

head.ce:title: A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science, East China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized for determination of paranitrophenol (4-NP) in water sample, where molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was incorporated in GQDs-based sensing system for the first time. A simple hydrothermal method was used to fabricate silica-coated GQDs. The final composite was developed by anchoring the MIP layer on the silica-coated GQDs using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as functional monomer and tetraethoxysilane as crosslinker. The combination of GQDs and MIP endows the composite with stable fluorescent property and template selectivity. Due to resonance energy transfer from GQDs (donor) to 4-NP (acceptor), the fluorescence of the MIP-coated GQDs composite can be efficiently quenched when 4-NP molecules rebound to the binding sites. The composite was applied to the detection of the non-emissive 4-NP and exhibited a good linearity in range of 0.02–3.00µgmL−1 with the detection limit of 9.00ngmL−1 (S/N=3). This work may open a new possibility for developing GQDs-based composite with selective recognition, and it is desirable for chemical sensing application.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the severely toxic substituted phenols, paranitrophenol (4-NP) can cause headaches, drowsiness, nausea and cyanosis through acute inhalation or ingestion. So the use of 4-NP should be strictly controlled and supervised. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has provided the allowed limit of 4-NP in drinking water at 60ngmL−1 (Wei et al., 2011). Various methods have been explored to detect 4-NP and applied in real samples, such as chromatographic techniques (Liu et al., 2007), electrochemical detection (Zhang et al., 2012c), chemiluminescence detection (Liu et al., 2010) and fluorescent monitoring (Catalin et al., 2001). Among them, the fluorescent method has several advantages such as a small amount of solvent consumption, time-saving measurement and simple sample pretreatment (Li et al., 2010a). The conventional fluorescent methods for detecting non-fluorescent 4-NP need inducers or derivatizations. Recently, some groups have been successfully explored new optical sensing system based on luminescent probe (such as ZnS:Mn2+ quantum dots), and provided a facial and label-free method for determining non-fluorescent analytes (Wang et al., 2009; Zhao et al., 2012).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as recently emerging carbon-based materials, are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm with a unique sp2 and sp3 hybrid structure (Dong et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2013). They have attracted considerable attention on account of their special advantages, such as low toxicity, high fluorescent activity, robust chemical inertness, and excellent water solubility (Tetsuka et al., 2012). Due to these merits, GQDs have been proved to be potential candidates in photovoltaic devices, sensing and imaging fields (Ran et al., 2013).

Molecular imprinting is a well-known methodology to fabricate materials that function as synthetic receptors with predetermined binding sites (Lin et al., 2012). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have notable features such as improved stability, effective cost, and rapid fabrication (Lian et al., 2012). Typically, MIPs are prepared via polymerization in the presence of template molecules followed by removal of the template. After templates are removed, the remaining cavities with complementary size, shape, and orientation of functionalities are left behind (Han et al., 2005; Lian et al., 2012). In recent years, surface molecular imprinting polymers attract more attention, due to the prominent advantages over traditional MIPs, such as enhanced mass transfer, rebinding percentage and high selectivity (Zeng et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2010).

Silica materials have promising structural properties for imprinting matrix, as their rigid structures are highly suitable for the formation of a delicate recognition site (Jung et al., 2010). Imprinted silica films show fast kinetic binding for the template due to their nanosized thickness. In addition, silica coating is proven to be an ideal method to protect the fluorescent QDs (such as Mn-doped ZnS QDs) since the silica shell is chemically inert and optically transparent (Liu et al., 2010). Several groups have fabricated molecularly imprinted silica film on the surface of semiconductor quantum dots (such as Mn2+ doped ZnS QDs, CdSe QDs, CdTe QDs) (Wang et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2012b). These optical sensors show special binding and selective recognition to the template molecules. Considering the known toxicological effect of heavy-metal-based QDs (such as CdTe, CdSe QDs) on humans and the environment, an eco-friendly fluorescent sensor based on MIP-coated GQDs was proposed for the first time in this paper.

Herein, a novel composite of MIP-coated GQDs was successfully fabricated and an eco-friendly fluorescent sensor was constructed to selectively detect 4-NP based on this composite. In the 4-NP sensing system, GQDs acted as fluorophores and imprinted silica film provided specific binding sites for analytes. The fluorescence of MIP-coated GQDs was greatly quenched when interacted with 4-NP. The results of experiments indicated that the MIP-coated GQDs composite combines the merits of imprinted silica materials and GQDs, showing fast kinetics, special binding sites, and improved fluorescence. The novel fluorescent sensor realized specific and selective detection of 4-NP from its interferents. Moreover, the MIP-coated GQDs sensor had a wider linear range and lower detection limit than some reported methods. The optical sensor was applied to the determination of 4-NP in real water samples with satisfactory results.

Graphite, sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), thionyl chloride (SOCl2), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), hydroquinone and phenol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China), triethylamine (TEA), benzene, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), and dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were obtained from SS Precision Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (>18MΩcm).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were performed on NEXUS670 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Model JEM 2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a model F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis spectra were measured on a Varian cary-100 spectrometer.

APTS-GO was prepared according to the previous reports (Li et al., 2010b; Xiao et al., 2007). Briefly, 0.2g GO was dispersed in 20.0mL SOCl2 and 4.0mL benzene. The mixture was stirred for 24h under 70°C at reflux. The obtained solid (GO–Cl) was washed with ultra-dried tetrahydrofuran (THF) three times and dried under vacuum at 50°C. In the next step, 0.1g GO–Cl was dispersed in 20.0mL DMF, then 0.25mL APTS and 0.25mL TEA were added into the flask. The mixture was heated to 120°C and stirred for 48h. Afterwards, the mixture was centrifuged and the obtained product was washed with DMF and anhydrous ethanol for two times, respectively. The final product was dried under vacuum at 50°C over night.

The silica-coated GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal route according to Ref. (Pan et al., 2010) with some modification. 80.0mg APTS-GO was dispersed in 36mL distilled water in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 36h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was filtered through a 0.22µm microporous membrane. Finally, the colorless solution was collected and suspended steamed. The final volume of the solution was about 4mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIP- and NIP-coated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The imprinting polymer was fabricated on silica-coated GQDs via a typical sol–gel polymerization as previously reported with some modifications (Wang et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2011b). 4-NP (20mg), anhydrous ethanol (20mL) and APTS (50µL) were added to a 50mL beaker and stirred for 30min. Then, 4mL silica-coated GQDs colloidal solution, 1.6mL CH3COOH (1M), 200µL TEOS was injected, and the mixture was kept stirring for another 15h. The resultant MIP-coated GQDs were centrifuged and washed with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water for two times, respectively. After the templates were removed, the obtained MIP-coated GQDs were redispersed in 8mL water under ultrasonication and kept refrigerated prior to use. The non-imprinted NIP-coated GQDs were prepared using the same method without adding any template molecules.

The fluorescent measurements were carried out with the excitation wavelength of 320nm. The stock solution of MIP-coated GQDs was diluted 60 times, by mixing with Na2CO3–NaHCO3 buffer solution (pH 9). 600µL above mixture was added to the cuvette, and then different amounts of 4-NP was injected. The final concentration of analyte can be known through simply calculation. The fluorescence intensity was measured immediately after fully mixing. The control experiments of NIP-coated GQDs were the same as that of MIP-coated GQDs.

The proposed procedure was applied to the determination of 4-NP in water samples collected from tap water and Yingtao river of East China Normal University. The real water samples were filtered through a 0.45μm microporous membrane to eliminate particulate matters, and stored in precleaned glass bottles before analysis. 600µL mixed solutions containing 540µL water samples and 60µL Na2CO3–NaHCO3 (1M) buffer were added to the cuvette, then different quantities of 4-NP were added to the mixture solution and the resulting water solutions were used for fluorescent analysis. The content of the 4-NP was determined by the standard addition method.

In order to improve the interaction between GQDs and molecularly imprinted silica matrix, GQDs have to be coated with a layer of silica before the sol–gel hydrolysis and condensation imprinted polymerization reaction (Mao et al., 2012). It means that the final composite was obtained by multi-steps. The major steps involved in the synthesis of MIP-coated GQDs are illustrated in Scheme 1. (i) Chemical modified GO with APTS (APTS-GO). APTS was used as a precursor of silica layer which formed in the following reaction. (ii) APTS-GO was cut into silica-coated GQDs via a simple hydrothermal approach. (iii) Surface imprinting polymer was obtained by sol–gel polymerization in the presence of silica-coated GQDs, using 4-NP as template, APTS as functional monomer and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as crosslinker. After the templates were extracted from the silica matrix, the MIP-coated GQDs with imprinted cavities were fabricated.

To ascertain the successful chemical modification in each synthesized procedure, FT-IR spectra of GO, GO-APTS, silica-coated GQDs and MIP-coated GQDs were performed and compared in Fig. 1(A), respectively. The most characteristic features in the FT-IR spectrum of GO (curve a) are the absorption bands corresponding to the C=O carbonyl stretching at 1723cm−1, and sp2 hybridized C=C in-plane stretching at 1629cm−1. The broad adsorption band between 3000cm-1 and 3700cm−1 can be assigned to O–H stretching vibration of –OH groups of GO (Valentini et al., 2012). The C–O vibrations of epoxy groups appear at 1055cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2011a). The FT-IR spectrum of APTS-GO sample (curve b) shows the disappearance of signal at 1723cm−1 observed in the GO, which indicates the fully reduction of GO. The broad absorption peak at 3421cm−1 representing N–H stretching, the peak at 1540cm−1 representing secondary amide N–H bending and C–N stretching, the peak at 1629cm−1 representing secondary amide C=O stretching, indicate the presence of amide bonding (–CO–NH–). The FT-IR absorption peaks at 1072cm−1 and 1120cm−1 represent Si–O–C bond and Si–O–Si bond, respectively (Lin et al., 2011). Other observed bands at 793cm−1 and 463cm−1 also show the presence of Si–O bond. These peaks suggest the successful coating of APTS onto the GO through chemical bonding. As shown in the FT-IR absorption spectrum of MIP-coated GQDs (curve d), peaks at Si–O–Si (at 1120cm−1) and Si–O–C (at 1072cm−1) become stronger compared with that of silica-GQDs (curve c). These results confirm the copolymerization of TEOS and APTS on the surface of silica-coated GQDs (Li et al., 2010b; Mao et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM characterization of silica-coated GQD, MIP-coated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of silica-coated GQD before and after imprinting polymerization are shown in Fig. 1(B and C). Silica-coated GQDs with average size of 25nm are smaller than MIP-coated GQDs of about 40–50nm in size. The comparison clearly demonstrates the successful synthesis of MIP-coated GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and photoluminescence properties of silica- and MIP-coated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of silica-coated GQDs are shown in Figure S1(A), the excitation spectrum with two sharp peaks are similar to that of GQDs reported by Pan et al. (2010). The two electronic transitions of 310nm (4.00eV) and 232nm (5.34eV) observed in the photoluminescence excitation spectrum can be regarded as transitions from the σ and π orbitals (highest occupied molecular orbitals, HOMOs) to the π⁎ orbitals (the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals, LUMOs). Silica-coated GQDs show a symmetric and strong fluorescence emission peak at 390nm when excited at 310nm.

The prepared MIP-coated GQDs show a wide range of UV–vis adsorption band from 200nm to 400nm in Figure S1(B). When excited at 320nm, the MIP-coated GQDs show a symmetric fluorescence emission peak at 400nm, which is slightly red-shifted comparing with that of silica-GQDs at 390nm. The slight red shift of MIP-coated GQDs can be ascribed to the attachment of imprinted polymer to the silica-coated GQDs surface (Li et al., 2010a).

The eco-friendly fluorescent MIP-coated GQDs composite can be further applied to efficiently 4-NP sensing. As shown in curve a and b of Fig. 2(A), after templates are removed by washing with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-coated GQDs (see curve b) could be restored to that of NIP-coated GQDs (see curve a). In addition, the fluorescence of MIP-coated GQDs without templates is quenched a lot when interacted with 4-NP molecules (see curve c).

Resonance energy transfer (RET) from MIP-coated GQDs (donor) to 4-NP molecules (acceptor) is suggested as the mechanism of fluorescent quenching phenomenon. As shown in curve a of Fig. 2(B), a remarkable UV–vis adsorption band of 4-NP is around 400nm in Na2CO3–NaHCO3 buffer solution (at pH 9.0). The absorption band of 4-NP greatly overlaps the emission spectrum of the MIP-coated GQDs shown in curve b. When 4-NP molecules rebind to the binding sites of MIP-coated GQDs, the energy transfer from donor to acceptor follows the path shown in Fig. 2(C). The excited-state electrons of MIP-coated GQDs return to ground state, the ground-state electrons of 4-NP thus transit to the excited states, due to the polar–polar resonance of donor and acceptor. The coupling transitions will result in the highly efficient quenching of MIP-coated GQDs fluorescence.

The pH effect on the fluorescence ratio (F 0/F−1) of MIP-coated GQDs was investigated by tuning pH from 4.0 to 11.0. Herein, F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the composite before and after the addition of 4-NP, respectively. As shown in Figure S2(A), the fluorescence ratio of the sensor to 4-NP increases with the pH increasing from 4.0 to 9.0, and then decreases slightly from pH 9.0 to 11.0. Thus, pH 9.0 was chosen as the optimum pH in the detection of 4-NP solution in this work. The results shown in Figure S2(B) indicate the stable fluorescence emission of MIP-coated GQDs at pH 9.0. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.05% was obtained by 6 times repeated detections.

The –NH2 groups at the surface of MIP-and NIP-coated GQDs acted as the binding sites to combine 4-NP through hydrogen bond interactions (Liu et al., 2010). Compared with the NIP-coated ones, the MIP-coated GQDs have more binding sites suitable for the template 4-NP due to an efficient imprinting effect. Therefore, both the prepared MIP- and NIP-coated GQDs showed fluorescence response to the template 4-NP ( Fig. 3(A and B)). The ratio of the K
sv,MIP and K
sv,NIP (K
sv,MIP and K
sv,NIP are the linear slopes of Fig. 3(C)) is defined as imprinting factor (IF), which is used to evaluate the specific recognition of the materials (Wang et al., 2009). Under optimum conditions, the IF (K
sv,MIP/K
sv,NIP) is 2.2, which indicates that MIP-coated GQDs could recognize the template molecules. The higher quenching efficiency of MIP-coated GQDs suggests its specific imprint binding affinity for 4-NP (Li et al., 2010a).

In order to illustrate the selectivity of MIP-coated GQDs toward 4-NP, control experiments were carried out to the other phenols with similar structure (such as hydroquinone and phenol), or similar optical chemical property (2-NP). As shown in Figure S3, all the fluorescence ratios (F 0/F-1) of analogs are smaller than that of 4-NP. The mechanism of quenching phenomenon should be considered from two aspects, optical properties and chemical structures of the analogs. The fluorescence is hardly influenced by hydroquinone and phenol due to their absolutely different absorption spectra from 4-NP (see Figure S4). In the six aromatic organic compounds, 2-NP has similar optical and chemical properties to 4-NP. However, the quenching of the system to the two similar compounds is different. The quenching to template molecule 4-NP is about three times of that to 2-NP. This proves the selectivity of the fluorescent sensor to the compounds which have the similar properties in the helping of imprinted effect. Based on the above results, MIP-coated GQDs can be used for selective analysis of 4-NP in the presence of other phenols. It further shows that the fluorescent senor could be applied to selective detection of 4-NP.

The characteristics of calibration curve and detection limit of the fluorescent sensor were investigated. We found that the fluorescence quenching in this system followed the Stern–Volmer equation (Wang et al., 2009),
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
c
where F
0 and F are the fluorescent intensities in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively, c is the concentration of 4-NP, and K
sv is the quenching constant of the quencher. To ascertain the information, the fluorescent sensor was exposed to a series of solutions and measurements were taken after fully mixing. As shown in Figure S5, the fluorescence intensity decreased notably when exposed to the increasing concentrations of template. The linear range of the calibration curve was obtained from 0.02 to 3.00µgmL−1 with a detection limit of 9.0ngmL−1 (S/N=3). The value is well below 60.0ngmL−1, which is the allowed limit in drinking water given by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Wei et al., 2011). It is worth to compare the performance of the fluorescent sensor based on MIP-coated GQDs composite with other analytical method reported previously. The determined values of the present study, together with the reported data for 4-NP monitoring are summarized in Table 1. Our work demonstrated wide linear range and low detection limit of the prepared fluorescent sensor based on MIP-coated GQDs.

Tap water and river water samples were collected from our laboratory and Yingtao river of East China Normal University, respectively. No response corresponding to 4-NP was observed in the real water samples. Furthermore, different quantities of 4-NP were added to the samples. A summary of the spiked concentration for each water sample and the corresponding analysis results are shown in Table S1. The concentrations of 4-NP in the spiked river samples determined by the developed method were in good agreement with those of 4-NP added. The quantitative recoveries ranged from 97.0% to 100.5%, which showed that the fluorescent sensor based on MIP-coated GQDs has the potential applicability for 4-NP detection in real samples.

In summary, we have prepared the composite of MIP-coated GQDs for the first time and successfully fabricated eco-friendly fluorescent sensor based on the composite for determining non-fluorescent 4-NP. The novel fluorescent sensor possesses prominent merits, such as stable fluorescence, eco-friendly property, rapid response and good recognition specificity. In addition, the MIP-coated GQDs sensor had a wider linear range and lower detection limit, which revealed a prospective application in the field of environmental monitoring and evaluation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175044, 21275055, 21277048). This work is supported the Programs of the Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (10JC1404000) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (11ZR1410700). We also greatly thank the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20100076110002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2013.09.022.
